structured_choices.data_source: Operational data from a single regional courier distribution hub spanning 24 consecutive months. Data includes: (1) hourly parcel intake counts, (2) sortation scan events with timestamps, (3) route assignment records, (4) dispatch times and exception codes, (5) local weather observations (precipitation, temperature, wind). All timestamps are in UTC and validated for monotonicity. Data quality: missing values in weather (<2%) are forward-filled; scan events with timestamp anomalies (future-dated or duplicates within 1 second) are removed (<0.5% of records). No data from multiple hubs is pooled.
structured_choices.label_definition: The target variable is next-day parcel delay volume, defined as the count of parcels with actual delivery time exceeding promised delivery time by ≥4 hours, aggregated over the calendar day (00:00–23:59 UTC) following the prediction date. Delays are determined from dispatch records and delivery confirmations available in operational systems within 48 hours of delivery attempt. Days with incomplete data (e.g., system downtime, missing delivery confirmations for >5% of dispatched parcels) are excluded from analysis.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split (no shuffling). The 24-month dataset is divided into three contiguous windows: (1) Months 1–16 (training set, ~480 calendar days), (2) Months 17–20 (validation set, ~120 days), (3) Months 21–24 (test set, ~120 days). This preserves chronological order and prevents future information leakage. The split points are chosen to fall at week boundaries to avoid breaking within-week seasonal patterns. Justification: Time series forecasting requires temporal splits because random shuffling would leak future delays into training, violate the causality assumption, and produce overoptimistic estimates of real-world performance. Retail logistics exhibits strong day-of-week, holiday, and promotional calendar effects; temporal splits respect these dependencies.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–16) before any model training begins. Scaling parameters (mean, std for numeric features), categorical encodings (one-hot or ordinal mappings), and imputation statistics are stored and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents validation and test data from influencing feature transformations, which would constitute a subtle form of leakage.
structured_choices.features: Feature set is divided into three groups: (A) Engineered temporal features: day-of-week (Monday–Sunday, one-hot encoded), is-holiday (binary, for national holidays and known promotional peak days like Black Friday, based on a curated calendar), day-of-year (sine/cosine encoding to capture annual seasonality), hour-of-day (sine/cosine encoding for 24-hour cycle). (B) Lagged operational features: hourly parcel intake count (lags: 1, 24, 168 hours, i.e., 1 hour, 1 day, 1 week prior), cumulative sortation scans in previous 6 hours (captures current workflow intensity), count of exception codes flagged in previous 12 hours (proxy for operational friction). (C) Weather features: same-day max/min temperature, precipitation (mm), wind speed (m/s), lagged 24 hours. All lags are computed within the training window; no future-dated features are used. Feature timestamps are validated: each feature value at time t uses only information available strictly before or at time t.
structured_choices.model: Gradient boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained to minimize squared error loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, number of rounds, regularization) are tuned via grid search over the validation set (see validation approach below). The model produces a point prediction of next-day delay count (numeric output, unbounded). No probabilistic calibration or quantile regression is applied at this stage.
structured_choices.baseline: Baseline model: exponential smoothing (ETS or equivalent ARIMA) fitted to historical delay counts using only the univariate time series of next-day delays. No external features (weather, scans, routes) are used. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (e.g., smoothing constants, differencing order) are selected via grid search on the same validation set, using the same evaluation metric. Both models are trained on the same training window and evaluated identically. Justification: The hypothesis claims that multi-feature gradient boosting outperforms a model using 'only historical delay totals.' A univariate time series baseline directly represents that claim. ETS/ARIMA is the standard forecasting baseline in logistics and is computationally efficient; it is a fair comparison because the hypothesis asserts that external features (weather, scans) provide *marginal* value beyond trend and seasonality.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) computed separately on days with high delay volume (top quartile, >75th percentile of next-day delay counts in the validation set) and days with low delay volume (bottom quartile, <25th percentile). The primary performance target is MAPE on high-volume days; this reflects the stated operational concern that 'performance should matter most when delays spike.' Operationally, errors during peak-demand periods drive staffing and vehicle allocation mistakes, so reducing forecast error on those days is the priority. Secondary evaluation uses absolute error (MAE) on high-volume days to check robustness to percentage-based metrics when delays are low (which can inflate MAPE). If average delay count on high-volume days is <5 parcels, absolute error is preferred.
secondary_metrics: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on high-volume days — penalizes large forecast errors more heavily, useful given heavy-tailed delay distributions.
Median Absolute Error (MedAE) on high-volume days — robust to outliers.
MAPE on the full test set (all days combined) — for reporting aggregate performance.
Quantile loss (pinball loss at τ=0.9) — forecasts the 90th percentile of delays, relevant for conservative staffing decisions.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both the gradient-boosted model and the baseline are tuned via 5-fold forward-chaining cross-validation on the training set. In forward-chaining, fold k uses data from months 1–(k) as training and the subsequent 3-month window as validation. This preserves temporal order and prevents look-ahead bias. The model checkpoint with the lowest MAPE on high-volume days (as computed across all 5 folds) is selected. The same fold structure and metric are used for both the proposed model and the baseline to ensure fair comparison. After model selection, the final model is retrained on the full training set (months 1–16) using the selected hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the held-out test set (months 21–24).
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 21–24) is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to report final performance metrics. No hyperparameter tuning, model selection, or threshold adjustment is performed on the test set. Test set results are reported for both the proposed gradient-boosted model and the baseline, together with 95% confidence intervals (computed via bootstrap resampling of test-set residuals, stratified by high/low volume quartile). The test set is not revisited after initial evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Leakage prevention: Feature timestamps are audited to ensure no information from day t is used to predict delays on day t. Specifically, sortation scans and route assignments must be timestamped before 20:00 UTC on day t−1 (sufficient buffer to ensure these are observable before the forecast is issued). Dispatch times that occur after forecast issuance are excluded. Exception codes are lagged by 12 hours to ensure operational staff have discovered and logged them before the forecast model runs. Weather forecasts (not observed weather) would be used in production; for this experiment, observed weather is used for proof-of-concept, but this is flagged as a limitation. Calendar confounds: Holiday effects and promotional peak days are explicitly modeled via the is-holiday feature; results are reported separately for holiday-adjacent periods vs. regular periods to isolate the improvement attributable to external features. Regime shifts: The 24-month window intentionally spans multiple holiday seasons and promotional cycles to test generalization; if performance degrades sharply in months 21–24 vs. months 17–20, this suggests model drift and is reported.

design_narrative:
To test this hypothesis rigorously, we would design a controlled time series forecasting experiment with explicit safeguards against leakage and confounding.

**1. Data and Labeling**

We would source 24 consecutive months of operational data from a single regional distribution hub. The dataset includes hourly parcel intake, sortation scans with exact timestamps, route assignments, exception codes, and local weather observations. The target label is next-day delay volume: the count of parcels with delivery time exceeding the promised time by ≥4 hours, aggregated over each calendar day. Delays are determined from dispatch and delivery confirmation records, available within 48 hours. Days with incomplete operational records (>5% missing deliveries) are excluded. Data quality is assessed upfront: missing weather values (<2%) are forward-filled; timestamp anomalies (duplicates, future-dated events) are removed.

**2. Temporal Split Strategy**

The 24-month window is divided into three contiguous blocks: months 1–16 (training, ~480 days), months 17–20 (validation, ~120 days), and months 21–24 (test, ~120 days). Split points are aligned to week boundaries to avoid breaking within-week seasonality. A temporal split is essential here because random shuffling would violate causality, leak future information into training, and produce misleading performance estimates. Retail logistics exhibits strong day-of-week and promotional calendar effects; temporal ordering preserves these dependencies and matches how the model will operate in production.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Three feature groups are constructed, all fit on the training set only: (A) Temporal: day-of-week (one-hot), is-holiday (binary, curated calendar), day-of-year and hour-of-day (sine/cosine encoded). (B) Operational lagged features: parcel intake count at lags 1, 24, and 168 hours; cumulative sortation scans in the past 6 hours; exception codes flagged in the past 12 hours. (C) Weather: same-day and lagged 24-hour max/min temperature, precipitation, wind speed. All feature timestamps are audited: no feature at time t uses information after time t. Sortation and dispatch data are required to be logged before 20:00 UTC on the day prior, ensuring the forecast model receives only causally valid inputs.

**4. Model and Baseline**

The proposed model is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the three feature groups above. The baseline is a univariate time series model (exponential smoothing or ARIMA) fit to next-day delay counts alone, without external features. This directly embodies the hypothesis: multi-feature gradient boosting versus historical delays only. Both models receive equal tuning effort via grid search on the validation set, ensuring fair comparison.

**5. Hyperparameter Tuning via Forward-Chaining Cross-Validation**

On the training set, we apply 5-fold forward-chaining cross-validation: fold k uses months 1–k (progressively larger windows) for training and the subsequent 3-month window for validation. This preserves temporal order and prevents look-ahead bias. The model checkpoint minimizing MAPE on high-volume days (top quartile, >75th percentile of next-day delays) is selected. After selection, the final model is retrained on the entire training set using the chosen hyperparameters.

**6. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) computed exclusively on high-volume days (top quartile of next-day delay counts). This aligns with the operational objective: forecast errors during peak-demand periods drive costly staffing and allocation mistakes, so we optimize for that regime. Secondary metrics include RMSE and Median Absolute Error on high-volume days (robustness to outliers and heavy tails) and MAPE on the full test set (aggregate performance).

**7. Test Set and Final Evaluation**

The test set (months 21–24) is held out and used exactly once, at the end. Both the proposed model and baseline are evaluated on the same test set using the primary and secondary metrics. Results include 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap on test residuals, stratified by volume quartile). Performance is reported separately for holiday-adjacent vs. regular periods to detect seasonal confounds.

**8. Confound Controls and Limitations**

Leakage is prevented by auditing all feature timestamps and ensuring forecasts use only pre-event information. Calendar effects are explicitly modeled. The experiment establishes whether multi-feature gradient boosting reduces forecast error on peak days in this hub over this 24-month period; it does not establish causality or generalization to other hubs or weather regimes without additional validation.
